critic_raw:
The methodology is generally well-constructed — the chronological split, group-aware ranking, time-safe feature generation, and explicit exclusion of outcome fields are all appropriate. However, I see several genuine methodological concerns worth examining.

**1. The 7-day drop rule creates a non-random missing-data mechanism that can systematically bias the training distribution.**

The claim is that dropping unresolved cases after 7 days removes noisy supervision. The problem is that resolution speed is not independent of alert type, attacker sophistication, or analyst workload. Slow-resolving alerts are disproportionately likely to be: (a) novel attack patterns with no prior playbook, (b) multi-stage intrusions where early alerts are ambiguous, or (c) alerts in queues with backlog pressure. All three of these categories are precisely the ones where a triage model most needs to learn. The dropped population is not missing-at-random — it is missing as a function of the very features (novelty, complexity, queue load) that predict triage difficulty. Training on the resolved-only subset will systematically underfit alerts that behave like slow-resolving cases, and NDCG on the resolved-only test set will not expose this because the test set has the same truncation bias. What would distinguish this from a real problem: compare the feature distributions of dropped vs. retained alerts; if high-novelty MITRE mappings, new rule IDs, or high-queue-load days are overrepresented in dropped cases, the bias is real. A practical partial fix is to include dropped cases with a censored or abstained label rather than discarding them.

**2. The baseline does not isolate what the hypothesis actually claims.**

The methodology states the baseline 'isolates both the ranking formulation and the added information source.' But a single baseline cannot simultaneously isolate two independent variables. If LambdaMART outperforms the classifier-ranked baseline, the gain could come from: (a) the ranking objective itself (LambdaMART vs. classification loss), (b) the richer feature set (historical feedback summaries, text features, entity recurrence), or (c) their interaction. To attribute gains to either factor, a second baseline is needed: LambdaMART trained on structured metadata only, without the additional feature families. Without this, any observed lift is unattributable, which weakens the scientific claim substantially. The description says ablations by feature family are run, but post-hoc ablations on a tuned model are not the same as a pre-specified matched baseline — feature interactions mean removing a feature family from a trained model does not cleanly estimate its marginal value at training time.

**3. Group construction (queue-day × business unit) may produce groups too small or too skewed to make NDCG@10 a stable signal.**

NDCG@10 requires at least 10 items per group to be non-trivially computed, and meaningful gradient signal in LambdaMART requires groups with both positive and negative labels. If many queue-day × BU slices have fewer than 10 alerts, or if high-severity alerts are rare within any given group (which is operationally likely — most SOC queues are majority-benign), then a large fraction of training groups will either have no positive labels (contributing zero to the ranking objective) or degenerate rankings. The methodology does not specify how degenerate groups are handled. This matters because LambdaMART's gradient computation is undefined or trivial for single-label groups, and silently dropping them would mean the model is trained on an unrepresentative subset of the queue distribution — likely the busiest or most alarmed queues. Evidence: report the distribution of group sizes and label density per group; if median group size is below 10 or median positive rate per group is below 5%, the primary metric and training objective are both compromised.

**4. The text feature time-gate (2-hour window) may produce near-zero signal in practice, creating a silent feature that looks populated but carries no information.**

The claim is that early ticket content provides useful signal. But in many SOC environments, initial ticket creation is automated (template-filled with the alert itself) and meaningful analyst commentary accumulates hours to days later. If the 2-hour window captures only auto-populated fields that mirror structured SIEM metadata already in the feature set, the text features contribute nothing new and the model will learn zero-weighted sparse features — which is not a problem by itself, but the ablation will then show 'text adds nothing' as a spurious null result rather than a true signal absence. More dangerously, if some BUs have faster ticket enrichment than others (e.g., a dedicated L1 analyst vs. automated triage), the text signal is present for some subgroups and absent for others, which the subgroup ablation may catch but only if the analyst-speed variation is correlated with BU boundaries. Evidence: measure the average token count and vocabulary entropy of early tickets by BU and alert family; if the 2-hour window consistently yields fewer than 10 non-boilerplate tokens, the feature family should be dropped or the gate reconsidered.

**5. Label policy-driven bias is acknowledged but not controlled for.**

The methodology explicitly notes it 'does not by itself prove... immunity to policy-driven label bias,' which is honest. But the concern is more specific than a general caveat: the graded relevance scheme maps label=2 to confirmed High/Critical incidents within 7 days. Incident severity classifications are themselves the output of analyst judgment, which is influenced by the queue order the analysts see. If the training period's queue was ordered by a prior scoring system, analysts were more likely to investigate — and therefore escalate — alerts that the prior system ranked highly. The label=2 density will be higher for alert types that the prior system favored, not just for objectively more dangerous alerts. This is survivorship bias in the label-generation process. The chronological split does not fix this because the bias exists throughout the 12-month training window. Evidence: compare label=2 rates for alert types that were historically ranked high by the prior system vs. alert types that were historically deprioritized; if there is a strong positive correlation between prior system rank and label=2 rate that cannot be explained by alert metadata alone, label contamination is present.

**Summary:** Issues 1 and 5 share a root cause — label generation is entangled with the operational context in ways that make the supervised signal systematically non-representative. Issue 2 is an experimental design gap that makes any positive result uninterpretable. Issues 3 and 4 are implementation-level concerns that could produce plausible-looking metrics on easy cases while silently failing on the cases the hypothesis targets. None of these are fatal individually, but issues 1, 2, and 5 together mean the experiment as specified cannot cleanly support its stated hypothesis.
all_issues_raised: The 7-day drop rule creates non-random missing data that systematically removes the hardest-to-triage alerts from training and evaluation, biasing both the model and the test set.
A single classifier-ranked baseline cannot isolate the ranking formulation from the richer feature set; a second LambdaMART baseline on structured metadata only is needed to attribute gains.
Queue-day × BU groups may be too small or too label-sparse for NDCG@10 to be a stable training signal or evaluation metric, and the handling of degenerate groups is unspecified.

The 2-hour text feature window may capture only auto-populated boilerplate that mirrors structured metadata, making text features silently uninformative and varying by BU in ways that confound subgroup ablations.

Graded relevance labels are derived from analyst incident classifications that were influenced by the prior queue ordering system, introducing survivorship bias into the label-generation process throughout the training window.
all_issues_adjudicated: The 7-day drop rule creates non-random missing data that systematically removes the hardest-to-triage alerts from training and evaluation, biasing both the model and the test set.
A single classifier-ranked baseline cannot isolate the ranking formulation from the richer feature set; a second LambdaMART baseline on structured metadata only is needed to attribute gains.
